summary:
Buddhism spread to the [[event:2091|Three Kingdoms of Korea]] in the 4th century AD, first reaching Goguryeo in 372 AD when the Former Qin state sent a monk and Buddhist texts to the Goguryeo court, then Baekje in 384 AD, and Silla somewhat later in the 5th–6th centuries. The transmission was deliberate and state-sponsored — Korean kings adopted Buddhism not primarily for personal spiritual reasons but because Buddhist institutions provided ideological legitimacy, literacy, administrative capacity, and connections to the sophisticated Chinese court culture they were emulating. Buddhist monasteries became centers of learning, medicine, and the arts.

The adaptation of Buddhism in Korea produced a distinctively Korean Buddhist tradition. Korean monks became important scholars in the broader East Asian Buddhist world — Wonhyo (617–686 AD) wrote over 80 commentaries on Buddhist scriptures that were studied across China and Japan, and developed an ecumenical approach to competing Buddhist schools. Uisang founded the Hwaeom (Huayan) school in Korea. Korean Buddhism maintained a strong emphasis on meditation and a synthesis of different Buddhist schools that would later influence the development of Korean Seon (Zen) Buddhism. Korea's role as a conduit between Chinese and Japanese Buddhism was crucial — Buddhist texts, monks, artisans, and art styles flowed from Korea to Japan, where Buddhism became the [[event:2096|state religion of the Yamato court]].
